expanded_description: A clear introduction to phloem tissue for secondary school biology. This covers how phloem transports sugar from leaves (where it is made) to the rest of the plant (roots, fruits, growing tips), the cells involved (sieve tubes and companion cells), the pressure flow mechanism that moves sap, and why phloem is essential for plant growth and crop yield.

core_concept.principle: Phloem is the plant's sugar transport system. It moves sugar (sucrose) from sources (where it is made or stored) to sinks (where it is used or stored). Transport is driven by pressure differences created when sugar is loaded into phloem at sources and unloaded at sinks.
core_concept.explanation:
Plants make their own food through photosynthesis. But not every part of the plant can photosynthesize. Roots are underground; fruits and seeds are often shaded; growing tips are busy making new cells. These parts need sugar delivered to them. Phloem is the tissue that does this delivery.

Phloem is made of living cells. This is different from xylem, which is made of dead cells. Phloem cells need to be alive because they have to actively load and unload sugar. The main conducting cells are called sieve tube elements. They are long, thin cells stacked end to end. The ends have porous walls called sieve plates. Sugar sap flows through these pores from one cell to the next.

Sieve tube elements are alive but they have no nucleus and few organelles. They cannot survive on their own. Next to each sieve tube element is a companion cell. The companion cell has a nucleus, ribosomes, and mitochondria. It provides everything the sieve tube needs—ATP, proteins, and signaling molecules. The two cells are connected by many tiny channels called plasmodesmata.

How does sugar move through phloem? The pressure flow hypothesis explains it. At a source (like a leaf), sugar is actively pumped into the sieve tubes. This makes the sap more concentrated. Water follows by osmosis, making the sap pressure higher. At a sink (like a root or fruit), sugar is removed. Water follows out, making the pressure lower. The pressure difference pushes the sap from the source to the sink. This is like water flowing from a high-pressure pipe to a low-pressure pipe.

Phloem can move sugar in either direction. In summer, leaves are sources, and roots are sinks—sugar moves down. In spring, before leaves emerge, roots are sources (starch broken down to sugar), and growing buds are sinks—sugar moves up. Different sieve tubes can carry sap in different directions at the same time.

Understanding phloem helps us understand how plants grow, how fruits develop, and how to improve crop yields. When we prune a tree or thin its fruit, we are changing source-sink relationships to get bigger, better fruit.

image.alt: A diagram showing phloem tubes running through a plant, with sugar moving from leaves (source) to roots and fruit (sinks)
image.caption: Phloem: Living tubes that transport sugar from sources (leaves, storage) to sinks (roots, fruits, growing tips). Pressure differences drive the flow.

sections.introduction:
Imagine you are a plant. You have leaves that capture sunlight and make sugar. But you also have roots underground that need sugar for energy. You have growing tips that need sugar to make new cells. You have fruits that need sugar to become sweet and attractive to animals. How do you get sugar from the leaves to all these places?

The answer is phloem. Phloem is the plant's sugar highway. It is a network of living cells that runs from the leaves to every part of the plant. It carries sugar (in the form of sucrose) from where it is made (sources) to where it is used or stored (sinks).

Phloem is different from xylem in important ways. Xylem is dead and moves water one way (up). Phloem is alive and moves sugar both ways (up or down) depending on where it is needed. Xylem moves by physical forces (cohesion-tension). Phloem moves by pressure differences created by loading and unloading sugar.

The phloem system is like a delivery network. Leaves are the factories. Roots, fruits, and growing tips are the customers. The phloem is the delivery trucks. The factories load sugar onto the trucks. The trucks drive to the customers. The customers unload the sugar. The trucks return to the factories for another load. This goes on all day, every day, for the life of the plant.

Phloem is essential for plant growth. Without phloem, roots would starve. Fruits would not develop. Seeds would not fill. The plant would not be able to grow tall or produce flowers and fruit. Understanding phloem helps us understand how plants grow, how to get bigger fruits, and how to breed better crops.

introduction_image.alt: A diagram showing sugar moving from leaves (source) through phloem to roots, fruit, and growing tips (sinks)
introduction_image.caption: Phloem transports sugar from leaves (where it is made) to roots, fruits, and growing tips (where it is used). This is how plants feed all their parts.

key_concept.definition: Phloem is living tissue that transports sugar (sucrose) from sources to sinks. Sieve tubes conduct the sap; companion cells provide support. Flow is driven by pressure differences from sugar loading and unloading.
key_concept.context:
Phloem has two main cell types. Sieve tube elements are the conducting cells. They are long, thin, and stacked end to end. They have porous end walls (sieve plates) that let sap flow through. They are alive but have no nucleus or ribosomes. Companion cells are next to each sieve tube element. They have nuclei, ribosomes, and mitochondria. They do the metabolic work for the sieve tube, providing ATP, proteins, and signaling molecules.

Sugar moves through phloem by pressure flow. At a source (like a leaf), sugar is loaded into the sieve tubes. This makes the sap more concentrated. Water follows by osmosis, increasing pressure. At a sink (like a root), sugar is unloaded. Water follows out, decreasing pressure. The pressure difference pushes the sap from the source to the sink.

Phloem sap is mostly water (70-90%) with dissolved sugar (10-30% sucrose). It also contains amino acids, hormones, and even RNA molecules that carry signals from one part of the plant to another. This long-distance signaling coordinates growth and responses to the environment.

image.alt: Diagrams of sieve tube elements (with sieve plates) and companion cells (with nucleus) connected by plasmodesmata
image.caption: Phloem cells: Sieve tubes (conducting, enucleate, sieve plates) + Companion cells (nucleus, support) connected by plasmodesmata. Sieve tubes need companion cells to survive.

sections.key_concept: Phloem has two main cell types. Sieve tubes conduct sugar sap; companion cells support them. Sugar moves by pressure flow: loading at sources creates high pressure; unloading at sinks creates low pressure; the pressure difference drives flow.

classifications.value: Understanding sieve tubes explains how sugar sap moves long distances through the plant.
characteristics: Long, thin cells (0.1-1 mm long, 10-50 µm wide) stacked end to end
Living at maturity but lack nucleus, ribosomes, and large vacuoles
Sieve plates: porous end walls (1-15 µm pores) that let sap flow between cells
P-proteins: proteins that seal sieve plates when the tube is damaged
Callose: a carbohydrate that can block sieve plates (seasonal, wound response)
classifications.behavior:
Sieve tube elements are the pipes of the phloem system. They stack end to end to form long tubes called sieve tubes. The end walls are called sieve plates. Sieve plates have many pores (like a strainer) that let sap flow from one cell to the next.

Sieve tube elements are alive, but they have no nucleus, no ribosomes, and few organelles. They rely on companion cells for everything they need. The two cells are connected by plasmodesmata—tiny channels that allow molecules to pass from one cell to the other.

If a sieve tube is damaged (by a cut or an insect bite), the plant needs to stop the sap from leaking out. Two things happen. First, P-proteins (special phloem proteins) aggregate and plug the sieve plate pores. This happens quickly, within seconds. Second, callose (a carbohydrate) is deposited around the sieve plate, sealing it more permanently. This prevents the plant from losing precious sap.

In perennial plants, callose is deposited seasonally. In fall, sieve plates are sealed as the plant prepares for winter. In spring, the callose is removed, and phloem transport resumes. This is why trees can survive winter without losing sap.

examples.process: Sieve Plates: The Porous End Walls
examples.explanation:
If you look at a sieve tube under an electron microscope, you will see that the end walls are not solid. They are full of holes—sometimes one big hole, sometimes many small holes. These are sieve pores.

In flowering plants, sieve plates often have many small pores (like a colander). In some plants, the pores are so large that the end wall is almost completely open. This allows sap to flow quickly from one cell to the next.

In conifers (pine, spruce), the sieve areas are on the side walls, not the end walls. This is because conifers do not have true sieve tubes. Their phloem is simpler.

The size and number of pores affect how fast sap can flow. More pores = faster flow. But more pores also mean more sap loss if the tube is damaged. Plants balance speed and safety.

image.alt: Diagrams of sieve plates with pores (simple and compound) connecting sieve tube elements
image.caption: Sieve plates: Porous end walls connecting sieve tube elements. More pores = faster flow but more sap loss if damaged. Plants balance speed and safety.

examples.process: P-Proteins and Callose: Sealing the Wound
examples.explanation:
Imagine you cut a plant stem. Sap will start to ooze out. But within seconds, the oozing stops. Why? The plant has sealed the phloem.

First, P-proteins respond. P-proteins are dissolved in the phloem sap. When the pressure drops (because of the cut), they aggregate and form a plug at the sieve plates. This is like a clot forming in a cut blood vessel. It happens in seconds.

Second, callose is deposited. Callose is a carbohydrate (a beta-1,3-glucan) that is produced by the plant and deposited around the sieve plates. It hardens and seals the pores permanently. Over hours or days, the callose can be removed if the wound heals.

In the fall, trees deposit callose in their phloem to prepare for winter. In the spring, they break down the callose and start transporting sugar again. This is why maple sap flows in early spring—the phloem is being reactivated, and pressure builds up.

image.alt: Diagrams showing P-proteins plugging sieve plates and callose sealing the wound
image.caption: Wound response: P-proteins aggregate quickly (seconds) to plug sieve plates. Callose deposited later (hours) seals permanently. Prevents sap loss.

classifications.value: Understanding companion cells explains how sieve tubes stay alive and how sugar is loaded into the phloem.
characteristics: Living cells with nucleus, ribosomes, mitochondria, and full organelle complement
Connected to sieve tubes by many plasmodesmata (tiny channels)
Three types: ordinary (metabolic support), transfer (apoplastic loading), intermediary (symplastic loading)
High metabolic activity: produce ATP, proteins, and RNA for sieve tubes
Load and unload sugar using transporters
classifications.behavior:
Companion cells are the brains and muscle behind the sieve tubes. They have everything sieve tubes lack: a nucleus to direct activities, ribosomes to make proteins, and mitochondria to make ATP.

Each sieve tube element has one or more companion cells next to it. The two cells are connected by many plasmodesmata—channels that allow molecules to move directly from one cell to the other. Through these channels, the companion cell sends ATP, proteins, and other molecules to the sieve tube. The sieve tube sends signals back to the companion cell about what it needs.

There are three types of companion cells. Ordinary companion cells provide metabolic support. They are found in many plants. Transfer cells have wall ingrowths that increase the surface area of their plasma membrane. This allows them to move more sugar into the phloem (apoplastic loading). They are common in plants with high photosynthetic rates. Intermediary cells are involved in symplastic loading. They convert sucrose into larger sugars (raffinose, stachyose) that cannot move back through the plasmodesmata. This traps sugar in the phloem.

Companion cells also load signaling molecules into the phloem. They can put messenger RNA (mRNA) into the sieve tubes. These mRNAs travel long distances and tell other parts of the plant what to do. For example, when a leaf is ready to flower, it sends a signal (FT protein or FT mRNA) through the phloem to the shoot tip, telling it to start making flowers.

examples.process: Transfer Cells: Wall Ingrowths for Sugar Loading
examples.explanation:
Transfer cells are specialized companion cells with bumpy walls. Under a microscope, you can see that the cell wall has finger-like projections (ingrowths) that increase the surface area of the plasma membrane. More surface area means more room for sugar transporters.

Transfer cells are common in the minor veins of leaves. Here, sugar is loaded into the phloem. The sugar comes from the mesophyll cells (where photosynthesis happens). It moves into the space between cells (apoplast). Then, transporters in the transfer cell membrane pump the sugar into the companion cell. From there, it moves into the sieve tube through plasmodesmata.

This system is efficient. It can load a lot of sugar quickly. It also allows the plant to control how much sugar goes into the phloem. When the plant needs to send more sugar to the roots or fruits, it can increase the activity of the transporters.

image.alt: A transfer cell with wall ingrowths (bumpy surface) and sugar transporters
image.caption: Transfer cells: Wall ingrowths increase surface area for sugar transporters. Load sugar from apoplast into phloem. High capacity, regulated loading.

examples.explanation:
Phloem does not just carry sugar. It also carries signals. These signals tell different parts of the plant what is happening elsewhere.

For example, when a leaf is infected by a pathogen, it sends a signal through the phloem to warn other leaves. The other leaves then activate their defenses before the pathogen reaches them.

When roots sense drought, they send a hormone (ABA) through the xylem to the leaves. But they also send signals through the phloem. These signals tell the leaves to close their stomata, conserving water.

When a plant is ready to flower, the leaves send a signal called FLOWERING LOCUS T (FT) through the phloem to the shoot tip. The FT protein (or the mRNA that makes it) travels to the shoot tip and tells it to start making flowers. This is how plants know when to bloom.

Scientists have discovered that phloem sap contains many different RNAs and proteins. These molecules are made in one part of the plant and travel to other parts, where they affect development and stress responses. The phloem is not just a sugar highway; it is also a communication network.

image.alt: A diagram showing signaling molecules (FT, RNAs, hormones) moving through phloem to coordinate growth and flowering
image.caption: Phloem signaling: mRNAs, proteins (FT), and hormones travel long distances. Coordinate flowering, stress responses, and development. Phloem is a communication network.

classifications.value: Understanding pressure flow explains how sugar can move from leaves to roots, from storage to shoots, and why phloem can transport in both directions.
characteristics: Source: where sugar is loaded into phloem (leaves, storage organs releasing reserves)
Sink: where sugar is unloaded from phloem (growing roots, shoots, fruits, storage organs accumulating reserves)
Loading: active transport of sugar into sieve tubes (uses ATP)
Unloading: removal of sugar from sieve tubes (may be active or passive)
Pressure gradient: high pressure at source, low pressure at sink, drives flow
classifications.behavior:
The pressure flow hypothesis was proposed by Ernst Münch in 1930. It is the accepted explanation for how phloem works.

At a source (like a leaf), sugar is loaded into the sieve tubes. This is done actively, using energy (ATP). The sugar concentration in the sieve tubes becomes very high (10-30% sucrose). This lowers the water potential, so water moves into the sieve tubes from the xylem by osmosis. The water makes the pressure inside the sieve tubes increase (up to 2-3 MPa).

At a sink (like a root or fruit), sugar is unloaded. It is used for growth, respiration, or stored as starch. As sugar is removed, the water potential in the sieve tubes rises, and water moves out into the xylem. This lowers the pressure in the sink end.

The result is a pressure difference: high pressure at the source, low pressure at the sink. This pressure difference pushes the sap from the source to the sink. This is like water flowing from a high-pressure pipe to a low-pressure pipe.

The beauty of this system is that it works in either direction. In summer, leaves are sources and roots are sinks—sap flows down. In spring, before leaves emerge, roots are sources (starch broken down to sugar) and buds are sinks—sap flows up. Different sieve tubes can carry sap in different directions at the same time.

Phloem flow is slower than xylem flow. Typical speed: 0.5-1 meter per hour. This is fast enough to deliver sugar to all parts of the plant, but slow enough that sap can be loaded and unloaded along the way.

examples.process: Source Loading: How Sugar Gets Into Phloem
examples.explanation:
In a leaf, sugar is made in the mesophyll cells (photosynthesis). How does it get into the phloem? There are two main pathways.

Symplastic loading: Sugar moves from cell to cell through plasmodesmata. It diffuses from the mesophyll into the phloem. This pathway does not use ATP for transport, but it requires the cells to be connected. In some plants (like squash), the companion cells convert sucrose into larger sugars (raffinose, stachyose) that cannot diffuse back. This traps the sugar in the phloem.

Apoplastic loading: Sugar moves out of the mesophyll cells into the space between cells (apoplast). Then, transporters in the companion cell membrane pump the sugar into the cell. This uses ATP (for the proton gradient that powers the transporter). This pathway is more energy-intensive but allows the plant to control how much sugar is loaded.

Both pathways result in high sugar concentration in the sieve tubes, which draws water in and increases pressure.

image.alt: Diagrams of symplastic loading (through plasmodesmata) and apoplastic loading (using transporters)
image.caption: Source loading: Symplastic (through plasmodesmata, passive) or Apoplastic (transporters, active). Both concentrate sugar in phloem, drawing water in and increasing pressure.

examples.process: Sink Unloading: How Sugar Gets Out
examples.explanation:
At a sink, sugar is removed from the phloem. This can happen in two ways.

Symplastic unloading: Sugar moves from the sieve tube into sink cells through plasmodesmata. This is passive—sugar flows down its concentration gradient. It is common in growing tissues where sugar is used quickly.

Apoplastic unloading: Sugar moves out of the sieve tube into the space between cells (apoplast). Then, transporters in the sink cell membranes pump the sugar in. This may be active or passive, depending on the concentration gradient.

Once sugar is in the sink cells, it is used. Some is used for respiration (to make ATP). Some is used to make new cell walls and other structures. Some is stored as starch (in roots, tubers, seeds) or as sugar (in fruits).

As sugar is removed, water follows out, and pressure decreases. This maintains the pressure gradient that drives flow from the source.

image.alt: Sugar moving from phloem into sink cells (root, fruit) by symplastic or apoplastic pathways
image.caption: Sink unloading: Sugar removed from phloem → used for growth, respiration, or storage → water follows → pressure decreases. Maintains pressure gradient for flow.

principles.principle: Pressure Flow Requires Energy for Loading, Not for Flow
principles.statement: Loading sugar into phloem at the source uses energy (ATP). The flow itself is passive, driven by the pressure gradient created by loading and unloading.
principles.application:
The pressure flow system is clever: it uses energy only where it is needed (at the sources and sinks), and the long-distance transport itself is free. This is like a water tower: you pump water to the top (uses energy), then let it flow down (free).

In plants, the energy is used by companion cells to load sugar into the sieve tubes. In apoplastic loading, ATP is used to maintain the proton gradient that drives SUT transporters. In symplastic loading, ATP may be used to make the larger sugars (raffinose, stachyose) that are trapped in the phloem.

Once the pressure gradient is established, the sap flows without additional energy. The pressure difference does all the work. This is why phloem can transport sugar long distances—meters or even tens of meters—without a heart.

If you block ATP production (with a poison), loading stops, and the pressure gradient collapses. Flow stops. This shows that the system needs energy for loading, not for flow.
principles.example:
If you cut a ring of bark (phloem) from a tree, sugar accumulates above the cut and is depleted below. This is a classic experiment called girdling. It shows two things. First, phloem transports sugar downward (from leaves to roots). Second, flow depends on continuity—break the pipe, and flow stops.

If you apply a metabolic poison to a leaf, it will stop loading sugar into the phloem. The pressure gradient will decrease, and flow to that leaf's sinks will slow. This shows that loading requires energy.
principles.analogy: Phloem transport is like a water slide. You pump water to the top (loading, uses energy). The water then slides down (flow, no energy). At the bottom, you collect the water (unloading, may use energy). The pump (source) and the drain (sink) create the pressure difference that makes the water flow.

image.alt: Diagram showing energy use at source (loading) and sink (unloading) but not for bulk flow
image.caption: Energy use: Loading (ATP) + Unloading (may use ATP) = Energy cost. Bulk flow = Passive (pressure-driven). ~10-20% of photosynthate used for transport.

principles.principle: Sources and Sinks Change with Season and Development
principles.statement: Which parts of the plant are sources and which are sinks changes over time. This allows the plant to allocate resources where they are most needed.
principles.application:
In the spring, before leaves emerge, a tree's roots and stems are sources. They have stored starch from the previous year, and they break it down into sugar. The sugar is transported upward to the buds, which are sinks. The buds use the sugar to make new leaves and flowers.

In the summer, the leaves are sources. They make sugar through photosynthesis. The sugar is transported to roots (for growth), fruits (for development), and storage organs (for next year).

In the fall, leaves are still sources, but now the priority is storage. Sugar is transported to roots, tubers, and other storage organs. The plant is getting ready for winter.

In the winter, the plant is dormant. There is little phloem transport. The sieve plates are sealed with callose.

This seasonal change in source-sink relationships is controlled by hormones, day length, and temperature. It allows the plant to survive winter and to allocate resources to reproduction when the time is right.
principles.example:
Maple syrup comes from phloem sap. In late winter and early spring, maple trees are tapped. The sap flows from the roots (where starch is stored) to the branches (where buds are about to open). The sap is collected and boiled down to make syrup. This is a seasonal source-sink reversal: roots are source, buds are sink.

In fruit trees, the fruit is a strong sink. It pulls sugar from the leaves. If the tree has too many fruits, each fruit gets less sugar, and they are small. Farmers thin the fruit (remove some) so the remaining fruits get more sugar and grow larger. This is managing source-sink relationships for better crop yield.
principles.analogy: Source-sink relationships are like a household budget. In spring, you spend savings (storage) on new growth. In summer, you earn income (photosynthesis) and spend it on expenses (roots, fruits). In fall, you save for winter. The plant, like a household, shifts between saving and spending depending on the season.

image.alt: Diagrams showing source-sink changes in spring (storage → buds), summer (leaves → roots/fruits), fall (leaves → storage)
image.caption: Seasonal changes: Spring (storage → buds), Summer (leaves → roots/fruits), Fall (leaves → storage). Plants shift resources based on need.

principles.statement: Crop yield depends on how much sugar the phloem can transport to the harvested parts (grains, fruits, tubers). Understanding phloem helps us breed better crops.
principles.application:
For a grain crop like wheat, the yield is the grain. The grain is a sink. It gets its sugar from the leaves (source) through the phloem. If the phloem cannot transport enough sugar, the grains will be small or empty.

Breeders have selected for traits that improve phloem transport. In the Green Revolution, semi-dwarf wheat varieties had shorter, stronger stems. This meant they could support more grain without falling over. But they also had better phloem transport to the grains. The result was doubled yields.

In fruit crops, farmers manage source-sink relationships by thinning fruit. Fewer fruits mean each fruit gets more sugar. This improves fruit size and quality.

In sugarcane, the harvested part is the stem, which stores sugar. The phloem transports sugar to the stem. Breeders have selected for varieties that store more sugar in the stem (stronger sink).

Understanding phloem helps us grow more food. By managing sources (fertilizer, irrigation) and sinks (pruning, thinning), we can improve yield. By breeding for better phloem transport, we can create varieties that produce more grain, fruit, or sugar.
principles.example:
The Green Revolution is the best example of how phloem transport affects yield. Traditional wheat varieties had tall stems. When farmers added fertilizer, the plants grew more leaves (more source) but also grew taller stems. The tall stems would fall over (lodge) under the weight of the grain. This limited yield.

Semi-dwarf wheat varieties (with Rht genes) had shorter, thicker stems. They could support more grain without falling over. But they also had stronger sinks (more grains per head) and better phloem transport to fill those grains. When farmers added fertilizer, the plants used the extra sugar to make more grain, not more stem.

The result was a doubling of wheat yields. This is the power of understanding source-sink relationships.
principles.analogy: Phloem transport is like the supply chain for a factory. The factory (source) makes the product. The trucks (phloem) deliver it to customers (sinks). If the trucks are slow or the roads are narrow, the customers do not get enough product. Improving the supply chain (phloem transport) increases the amount that reaches the customers (yield).

image.alt: Comparison of traditional wheat (tall, lodging) and semi-dwarf wheat (short, high yield)
image.caption: Crop yield: Semi-dwarf wheat has stronger stems and better phloem transport to grains. Result: doubled yields. Phloem transport is key to food production.

components.definition: Phloem is made of sieve tubes (conducting), companion cells (support), fibers (structural support), and parenchyma (storage). These work together to transport sugar from sources to sinks.
components.interpretation: The flow of phloem sap depends on the pressure difference between source and sink, the radius of the sieve tubes (wide tubes flow faster), the viscosity of the sap (thick sap flows slower), and the distance the sap must travel. Plants optimize these factors for their environment.

image.alt: A diagram of phloem tissue showing sieve tubes, companion cells, fibers, and parenchyma
image.caption: Phloem components: Sieve tubes (conducting), Companion cells (support), Fibers (strength), Parenchyma (storage). All work together to transport sugar.

categories.type: Phloem Sap: What Is in the Tubes
categories.description: Phloem sap is a complex mixture. It is mostly water and sugar, but it also contains amino acids, hormones, and even RNA molecules.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Phloem sap is about 70-90% water. The dissolved solids are mostly sugar (10-30% sucrose). This makes the sap thick and sweet.

Why sucrose? Sucrose is a disaccharide (glucose + fructose). It is stable (does not react with other molecules), soluble (can be concentrated), and energy-dense. It is perfect for long-distance transport.

In addition to sugar, phloem sap contains:

Amino acids (0.1-1%): These are used to make proteins. Glutamine and asparagine are common.

Hormones: Auxin, cytokinin, ABA, and gibberellins travel in phloem. They coordinate growth and responses to stress.

RNAs: Messenger RNAs and small interfering RNAs travel long distances. They carry signals from one part of the plant to another. For example, the FT mRNA that triggers flowering travels in phloem.

Proteins: Several proteins are found in phloem sap. Some are involved in sealing wounds (P-proteins). Others are signaling molecules.

Ions: Potassium, calcium, magnesium, and phosphate are present.

The composition of phloem sap changes with the season and with the plant's needs. In spring, when storage organs are breaking down starch, the sap may have more sugar. In fall, when leaves are senescing, the sap may have more amino acids as nitrogen is moved out of the leaves.

examples.process: Why Sucrose Is the Transport Sugar
examples.explanation:
Why don't plants transport glucose or fructose? Glucose is a reducing sugar—it can react with proteins and cause damage. Sucrose is a non-reducing sugar—it does not react with proteins. This makes it safe for long-distance transport.

Sucrose is also more soluble than glucose. You can have a 30% sucrose solution; a 30% glucose solution would be much thicker and harder to move.

Sucrose is energy-dense. It contains two sugar molecules, so it delivers twice the energy per molecule as glucose.

Some plants have evolved other transport sugars. In cucurbits (squash, pumpkin), the phloem transports raffinose and stachyose—larger sugars that are part of the polymer trapping mechanism. In some trees, sugar alcohols (sorbitol, mannitol) are transported. But for most plants, sucrose is the transport sugar of choice.

image.alt: Chemical structure of sucrose (glucose + fructose) compared to glucose
image.caption: Sucrose: Stable (non-reducing), highly soluble, energy-dense. Ideal for long-distance transport. Most plants use sucrose; some use raffinose or sugar alcohols.

categories.description: Sugar must cross membranes to get into and out of phloem. Special proteins called transporters do this job.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Two families of transporters are important for phloem transport.

SUT transporters (Sucrose Transporters) move sucrose into cells. They use a proton gradient to do this (H⁺ symport). The H⁺-ATPase pump uses ATP to move H⁺ out of the cell, creating a gradient. The SUT transporter uses this gradient to pull sucrose in. This is active transport—it uses energy.

SWEET transporters move sucrose out of cells. They are facilitated diffusion transporters—they let sucrose flow down its concentration gradient. They do not use energy directly. SWEET transporters are involved in loading (moving sucrose from mesophyll to apoplast) and unloading (moving sucrose from phloem to sink cells).

These transporters are regulated. Their expression changes with the plant's needs. When the plant needs to send more sugar to the roots, it may make more SUT transporters in the leaves. When the plant is stressed by drought, it may close down transporters to conserve sugar for essential processes.

Pathogens have learned to hijack these transporters. Some bacteria (Xanthomonas) inject proteins (TAL effectors) into plant cells that turn on SWEET genes. This causes the plant to pump sugar into the apoplast, where the bacteria can eat it. Resistant plants have mutations that prevent this manipulation.

examples.explanation:
SUT transporters are like little pumps that move sugar into cells. They are found in companion cells and other phloem cells.

How do they work? The H⁺-ATPase pump moves H⁺ out of the cell, making the outside more acidic (H⁺-rich) and creating an electrical gradient. The SUT transporter binds one H⁺ and one sucrose molecule from the outside. When the H⁺ moves into the cell (down its gradient), it pulls the sucrose in with it. This is like a car being pulled by a rope attached to a truck—the H⁺ gradient provides the pulling power.

This system can concentrate sucrose inside the cell up to 1000 times higher than outside. That is why phloem sap can be 10-30% sucrose.

If the H⁺-ATPase is inhibited, the gradient collapses, and loading stops. This is why phloem transport requires energy (ATP).

image.alt: Diagram of SUT transporter using H⁺ gradient to move sucrose into the cell
image.caption: SUT transporters: Use H⁺ gradient (created by H⁺-ATPase) to pump sucrose into phloem. Active transport. Can concentrate sucrose 1000×.

examples.explanation:
SWEET transporters are like doors that let sugar out of cells. They are found in many plant tissues.

In leaves, SWEET transporters move sugar from mesophyll cells into the apoplast (the space between cells). This sugar is then picked up by SUT transporters in the companion cells and loaded into the phloem.

In sinks, SWEET transporters may move sugar from the phloem into the apoplast, where other transporters pick it up for the sink cells.

SWEET transporters are facilitated diffusion carriers. They do not use energy directly. They just let sugar flow down its concentration gradient. If the concentration inside is higher than outside, sugar will flow out. If the concentration outside is higher, sugar will flow in.

Bacteria have learned to turn on SWEET genes. When Xanthomonas bacteria infect a plant, they inject proteins (TAL effectors) that bind to the plant's DNA and activate SWEET genes. The plant then pumps sugar into the apoplast, where the bacteria can eat it. This is how the bacteria get their food. Resistant plants have mutations in the SWEET promoter that prevent the bacterial protein from binding.

image.alt: Diagram of SWEET transporter letting sugar out of cell (facilitated diffusion)
image.caption: SWEET transporters: Facilitated diffusion (no energy). Let sugar out of cells. Pathogens hijack them to get sugar for themselves. Resistant plants block this hijacking.

worked_examples.title: Understanding Pressure Flow with Numbers
worked_examples.problem: A plant has a source pressure of 2.0 MPa and a sink pressure of 0.5 MPa. The distance from source to sink is 1 m. The sieve tube radius is 10 µm, and the sap viscosity is 1.5× that of water. (a) What is the pressure gradient? (b) How does the flow rate change if the radius doubles? (c) How does the flow rate change if the viscosity doubles?

step_by_step.calculation: Pressure difference = 2.0 - 0.5 = 1.5 MPa. Pressure gradient = 1.5 MPa / 1 m = 1.5 MPa/m.

Flow rate is proportional to radius to the 4th power (r⁴). If radius doubles (10 µm to 20 µm), flow increases by 2⁴ = 16 times. This is why wide sieve tubes are so efficient—small changes in radius have huge effects on flow.

Flow rate is inversely proportional to viscosity. If viscosity doubles, flow halves. This is why plants do not transport very concentrated sugar solutions—thick sap flows slowly. There is a trade-off: higher sugar concentration means more sugar per volume, but slower flow.

step_by_step.explanation: Summarize the factors affecting phloem transport.
step_by_step.calculation: Flow rate depends on: pressure gradient (bigger difference = faster flow), sieve tube radius (wider = much faster), sap viscosity (thicker = slower), and distance (longer = slower). Plants optimize these factors for their environment.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Pressure gradient = 1.5 MPa/m. (b) Doubling radius increases flow 16×. (c) Doubling viscosity decreases flow by 50%. This shows why plants have wide sieve tubes for fast growth and narrow tubes for safety, and why they balance sugar concentration (more carbon per volume) against flow rate.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Flow rate ∝ pressure gradient × radius⁴ / (viscosity × length). This is the physics of phloem transport. Wide tubes = fast flow (good for fast-growing plants). Narrow tubes = slow but safe (good for drought-tolerant plants). Thick sap = more sugar per volume but slower flow.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: Sugar from Leaf to Root
sections.introduction: A sugar molecule made in a leaf travels through phloem to a root, where it is used for growth. Understanding this journey reveals how the pressure flow system works.

process_flow.description: Photosynthesis produces sugar in mesophyll cells.
process_flow.mechanism: Chloroplasts in mesophyll cells capture light energy and fix CO₂ into sugar. Sugar (as triose phosphates) is exported from chloroplasts and converted to sucrose in the cytosol.
process_flow.result: Sucrose produced; ready for export to phloem.

image.alt: Sucrose being made in mesophyll cells from photosynthesis
image.caption: Step 1: Sucrose made in leaf mesophyll (photosynthesis). Ready to be loaded into phloem.

process_flow.description: Sucrose moves from mesophyll to companion cells and sieve tubes.
process_flow.mechanism: Sucrose moves to apoplast via SWEET transporters, then into companion cells via SUT transporters (active, uses ATP). From companion cells, sucrose moves into sieve tubes through plasmodesmata. Loading increases sugar concentration in sieve tubes, lowering water potential. Water enters from xylem by osmosis, increasing pressure.
process_flow.result: High sugar concentration in sieve tubes; high pressure (2-3 MPa); sap ready to flow.

image.alt: Sucrose moving from mesophyll to companion cell to sieve tube
image.caption: Step 2: Sucrose loaded into phloem (active transport, uses ATP). Water follows → pressure increases. Sap ready to flow.

process_flow.description: Pressure difference pushes sap through sieve tubes to the root.
process_flow.mechanism: High pressure at source (leaf) pushes sap toward lower pressure at sink (root). Sap moves through sieve tubes at 0.5-1 m/hour. Flow is passive—no energy needed once pressure gradient is established.
process_flow.result: Sucrose delivered to root; pressure gradient maintained by continuous loading/unloading.

image.alt: Sap flowing through sieve tubes from leaf (high pressure) to root (low pressure)
image.caption: Step 3: Pressure difference pushes sap through sieve tubes. Passive flow (no energy). Sap moves 0.5-1 m/hour.

process_flow.description: Sucrose exits phloem and is used for root growth or stored.
process_flow.mechanism: Sucrose moves from sieve tubes to root cells via symplastic (plasmodesmata) or apoplastic (transporters) pathways. In root cells, sucrose is used for respiration (ATP), cell wall synthesis, or converted to starch for storage. As sucrose is removed, water follows out, decreasing pressure. Low pressure at sink maintains gradient.
process_flow.result: Sucrose used for root growth; water returns to xylem; low pressure maintained; cycle continues.

image.alt: Sucrose moving from sieve tube into root cells, used for growth or storage
image.caption: Step 4: Sucrose unloaded at root → used for growth or stored → water follows → pressure decreases. Maintains pressure gradient for continuous flow.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Source-Sink Dynamics and Agriculture
sections.definition: Understanding source-sink relationships helps farmers improve crop yield. By managing sources (fertilizer, irrigation) and sinks (pruning, thinning), they can direct more sugar to the harvested parts.

process_types.type: Sink Strength: Why Some Fruits Grow Faster
process_types.energy_profile: Sink strength is the ability of a sink to attract sugar. Strong sinks get more sugar; weak sinks get less.
process_types.direction: Strong sink (developing seed) → attracts more sugar → grows faster
process_types.function: Plants allocate resources to the strongest sinks to maximize reproductive success.
goals: Abort weak sinks when resources are limited
process_types.energy_source: Sink strength is determined by metabolic activity (how fast sugar is used) and transporters (how efficiently sugar is taken up).

primary_example.explanation:
Fruit trees often produce more fruit than they can support. Each fruit is a sink. They compete for sugar from the leaves. If there are too many sinks, each gets less sugar, and the fruits are small.

Farmers thin the fruit—they remove some of the small fruits early in the season. This reduces sink competition. The remaining fruits get more sugar and grow larger. The result is bigger, better-quality fruit.

This works because the total amount of sugar from the leaves (source) is fixed. If there are fewer sinks, each sink gets more. It is like a pie: if you have more people to feed, each gets a smaller slice. Fewer people = bigger slices.

Thinning is common in apples, pears, peaches, and citrus. It is a way to manage source-sink relationships for better yield.

image.alt: Apple tree with many small fruits (before thinning) and fewer larger fruits (after thinning)
image.caption: Fruit thinning: Removing some fruits reduces sink competition. Remaining fruits get more sugar → larger, higher-quality fruit. Managing source-sink relationships improves yield.

image.alt: Diagram showing strong sinks (developing seeds) getting more sugar than weak sinks
image.caption: Sink strength: Developing seeds are strong sinks. They attract more sugar than leaves or stems. This prioritizes reproduction.

process_types.energy_profile: Crop yield can be limited by the source (not enough photosynthesis) or by the sink (not enough storage capacity). Knowing which is limiting helps breeders improve yield.
process_types.direction: Source-limited (increase photosynthesis) or Sink-limited (increase storage) → Different breeding strategies
process_types.function: Identify what limits yield to target improvement efforts.
goals: Increase grain/fruit number or size if sink-limited
Balance source and sink for maximum yield
process_types.energy_source: Source: sunlight, CO₂, water, nutrients. Sink: grain number, fruit size, storage capacity.

primary_example.explanation:
Traditional wheat varieties were sink-limited. They had tall stems and small grain heads. When farmers added fertilizer, the plants grew taller (more leaves, more source) but also grew taller stems. The stems could not support the weight of the grain, and the plants fell over (lodging). The yield was limited by the sink—the plant could not support more grain.

Semi-dwarf wheat varieties (with Rht genes) were different. They had short, thick stems. They could support more grain without falling over. When farmers added fertilizer, the plants put the extra sugar into grain (more grains per head, larger grains). The sink was no longer limiting. The result was doubled yields.

This is a classic example of identifying and fixing sink limitation. The source (photosynthesis) was not the problem; the sink (ability to support and fill grain) was. By breeding for stronger sinks, breeders dramatically increased yield.

image.alt: Comparison of traditional wheat (tall, lodging) and semi-dwarf wheat (short, high yield)
image.caption: Green Revolution: Traditional wheat (sink-limited, lodging) → Semi-dwarf wheat (stronger sink, higher yield). Fixing sink limitation doubled yields.

image.alt: Diagram showing source-limited (small leaves) and sink-limited (small grains)
image.caption: Yield limitation: Source-limited (increase photosynthesis) or Sink-limited (increase storage). Breeders identify the limiting factor to target improvement.

sections.relationship: Sink strength and source-sink limitation are two sides of the same coin. Sink strength determines which sinks get resources. Source-sink limitation determines whether yield is limited by supply (photosynthesis) or demand (storage). Understanding both helps farmers and breeders improve crop yield.

sections.purpose: These are the essential vocabulary terms for understanding phloem. Knowing these terms will help you describe how plants transport sugar.

activation.definition: The terms below cover the most important concepts in phloem biology. Each term is defined simply, with context to help you remember its significance.
activation.effect: Mastering these terms will allow you to discuss how plants move sugar, how they grow, and how we improve crop yields.

image.alt: A labeled diagram of phloem tissue with key terms: sieve tube, companion cell, sieve plate, plasmodesmata
image.caption: Key phloem terms: Knowing these structures helps you understand how sugar moves and how phloem works.

key_properties.explanation: The plant tissue that transports sugar (sucrose) from sources to sinks. Made of living sieve tubes and companion cells.
The conducting cell of phloem. Long, thin, stacked end to end. Lacks nucleus and ribosomes at maturity. Connected by sieve plates.
A living cell next to a sieve tube element. Provides metabolic support (ATP, proteins, RNA). Connected by plasmodesmata.
The porous end wall of a sieve tube element. Allows sap to flow from one cell to the next. Pores can be sealed by P-proteins and callose.
Proteins in phloem sap that aggregate and plug sieve plates when the tube is damaged. Seal the wound quickly.
A carbohydrate deposited around sieve plates to seal them. Used for seasonal dormancy (fall) and wound healing.
The explanation for how phloem works. Sugar loaded at source → water enters → pressure high. Sugar unloaded at sink → water exits → pressure low. Pressure difference drives flow.
Where sugar enters the phloem. Usually mature leaves (photosynthesis) or storage organs releasing reserves (spring).
Where sugar leaves the phloem. Growing roots, shoots, fruits, and storage organs accumulating reserves.
The main transport sugar in most plants. A disaccharide (glucose + fructose). Stable, soluble, energy-dense.
Sucrose transporter. Moves sucrose into cells using a proton gradient. Active transport (uses ATP). Found in companion cells.
Sucrose efflux transporter. Moves sucrose out of cells by facilitated diffusion (no energy). Found in mesophyll and sink cells.
Sugar moves out of mesophyll cells into the apoplast (space between cells), then is taken up by companion cells via SUT transporters. Uses ATP.
Sugar moves from cell to cell through plasmodesmata. Does not use ATP for transport. In some plants, sugar is converted to larger molecules (raffinose) that are trapped.
The ability of a sink to attract sugar. Determined by how fast sugar is used (metabolism) and how efficiently it is taken up (transporters).
Removing a ring of bark (phloem) from a tree. Sugar accumulates above the cut; roots below starve. Demonstrates that phloem transports sugar downward.
The balance between sugar production (sources) and sugar use (sinks). Changes with season and development. Determines growth and yield.

summary:
Phloem is the plant tissue that transports sugar (sucrose) from sources (where it is made or stored) to sinks (where it is used or stored). It is made of living sieve tube elements (which conduct sap) and companion cells (which provide metabolic support). Sieve tubes are connected by sieve plates (porous end walls). P-proteins and callose seal sieve plates when the tube is damaged.

Sugar moves through phloem by pressure flow. At a source, sugar is loaded into sieve tubes (active transport, uses ATP). Water follows by osmosis, increasing pressure. At a sink, sugar is unloaded. Water follows out, decreasing pressure. The pressure difference pushes the sap from source to sink. Flow is passive once the pressure gradient is established.

Loading can be symplastic (through plasmodesmata) or apoplastic (using SUT transporters). SUT transporters use a proton gradient (H⁺-ATPase) to pump sugar into companion cells. SWEET transporters let sugar out of cells by facilitated diffusion. Some plants (cucurbits) use polymer trapping: they convert sucrose to larger sugars (raffinose, stachyose) that cannot diffuse back.

Source-sink relationships change with season and development. In spring, storage organs are sources and buds are sinks. In summer, leaves are sources and roots/fruits are sinks. In fall, leaves are sources and storage organs are sinks. Strong sinks (developing seeds) get more sugar than weak sinks.

Understanding phloem helps improve crop yield. Fruit thinning reduces sink competition, making remaining fruits larger. The Green Revolution bred semi-dwarf wheat with stronger sinks (more grains) that could support higher yields. Managing source-sink relationships is key to agriculture.

Phloem also carries signals—hormones, RNAs, and proteins—that coordinate growth and stress responses. The FT protein (flowering signal) travels through phloem from leaves to shoot tips, triggering flowering.

Understanding phloem helps us understand how plants grow, how they allocate resources, and how we can improve crop yields for food security.
